items.title: 'No Israeli security without Palestinian freedom; no Palestinian freedom without Israeli security'
items.description: François Picard is pleased to welcome Dr. Gershon Baskin, Middle East Director of the International Communities Organisation and former hostage negotiator. One thousand days after the outbreak of the Gaza war, Gershon Baskin shares a stark assessment of Israel's political and strategic trajectory. Rather than framing the anniversary solely as a military milestone, he argues that it represents a profound failure of political leadership, strategic planning and institutional accountability.

items.title: 'If Ukraine falls, this will be the end of the European Union and the end of Europe's future'
items.description: Nadia Massih is pleased to welcome Dr. Volodymyr Yermolenko, Ukrainian philosopher, journalist, author and President of PEN Ukraine. According to Dr Yermolenko, the war is not simply a military confrontation, Europe's future hangs in the balance. Speaking hours after a devastating missile attack on Kyiv, he juxtaposes the intimate experience of civilian vulnerability with a broader strategic analysis, arguing that Russia's continued attacks on residential areas reflect military frustration rather than momentum. He contends that Ukraine's comparative advantage lies in asymmetric warfare, technological innovation, and the mobilisation of civil society, while warning that European support is not merely an act of solidarity but a matter of continental self-preservation.

items.description: Delano D'Souza is pleased to welcome Scott Lucas, Professor of International Politics at the University College Dublin. Lucas argues that the centre of gravity has fundamentally shifted. And he highlights the growing divergence between American diplomatic objectives, Israeli territorial ambitions, and Iranian efforts to secure economic recovery after a devastating conflict. Rather than depicting the current ceasefire as a pathway towards peace, Lucas characterises it as an unstable equilibrium sustained by mutual restraint, unresolved ambitions, and competing visions of regional order.

items.description: Across the continent, rising temperatures are driving a surge in demand for cooling and water, pushing already fragile electricity grids to the breaking point. African countries must modernize aging grids while aggressively scaling off-grid solutions

items.title: Cause of wrongly-positioned aircraft control in Shellharbour crash unknown
items.description: Investigators are unable to work out why a take-off control was wrongly-positioned leading to a fatal plane crash at Shellharbour Airport last October.

items.title: Iran will not cooperate with other countries to de-mine Strait of Hormuz
items.description: The Iranian Foreign Ministry said in a statement that, according to the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding (MoU), the mine clearance work in the Strait of Hormuz will be carried out by Iran alone, and Iran will not cooperate with any other country on this matter.

items.title: Ebola outbreak: UN projects $3.6 billion cost for Africa in worst case
items.description: In a report released Tuesday, the United Nations Development Programme outlined several scenarios for the economic cost of the ongoing Ebola virus outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda. In the worst case, the epidemic could lead to a $3.6 billion cost for the African continent.

items.title: Argentina's economy contracted in April as uneven growth persists
items.description: Economic activity fell 1.5% from March; From a year ago, gross domestic product proxy grew 1.6%. Leer más

items.title: 9 children dead in Egypt after motorised tricycle plunges into canal
items.description: Relatives waited outside Abu Tig hospital in Egypt's southern Assiut province on Tuesday for the bodies of nine children killed when a motorised tricycle plunged into a canal. The victims were aged between 10 and 17.

items.title: At least nine killed as explosive device goes off in cafe in Syria's Damascus

items.title: Russia launches attacks on Kyiv, killing at least 27 and injuring scores
items.description: Ballistic and cruise missiles and drones were used in what Kyiv's mayor, Vitali Klitschko, said was the largest attack on the city since the war began.

items.title: WHO declares cruise-linked hantavirus outbreak officially over
items.description: WHO declares cruise-linked hantavirus outbreak over as final exposed person completes quarantine and tests negative.

items.title: British PM Rishi Sunak avoids wipeout in key local elections
items.description: By-elections were seen as an indicator of the two main parties' prospects at a time when voters are struggling with high inflation, strikes and rising mortgage rates.

items.title: Hospital fire in northern Germany kills two
items.description: A fire at a hospital in the northern German town of Ludwigslust has killed two people. Many patients and staff were evacuated from the facility.

items.title: Floods kill 59 in Ivory Coast since mid-May: Government

items.title: Fact check: Racist German fan petition story false
items.description: It has been claimed that following Germany's World Cup exit, some fans launched a petition seeking to ban "African" and "Muslim" players from the national team — and that DW reported the story. This is not true.

items.title: Canadian boy, 11, dies of rabies after waking to bat on his face
items.description: Rabies infections are rare in Canada. There have been 28 human deaths due to rabies in the country since 1924.

items.description: "The body of the martyred Leader of the Islamic Revolution has arrived at the Imam Khomeini Grand Mosalla," IRNA wrote on Telegram, using the complex's official name

items.title: Thailand: Child crashes vehicle into group of monks
items.description: An 11-year-old boy crashed a pickup truck belonging to his parents into a group of monks on a pilgrimage, police said. Eight were killed in the incident and 14 more were hospitalized in Mukdahan province.

items.title: Residents of Ethiopian town forced to kill hundreds of their own dogs after rabies deaths
items.description: Some residents reluctantly hung their dogs to avoid being arrested or fined, locals tell the BBC.

items.title: Sudan's RSF committed ethnic cleansing, Amnesty report alleges
items.description: Sudan's paramilitary Rapid Support Forces committed crimes against humanity and ethnic cleansing during their campaign to seize El-Fasher between 2024 and 2025, according to a new report from Amnesty International released on Wednesday.

items.title: Former Olympian indicted for allegedly vandalising Washington Reflecting Pool
items.description: US authorities charged him with property destruction after renovations championed by President Donald Trump appeared to go awry.

items.title: Spain records more than 1,000 heat-related June deaths
items.description: The figure was more than double the 407 deaths that were attributed to heat in June 2025, Spain's hottest June since records started being kept, according to the national weather agency Aemet

items.title: UK uncovers international network of men drugging and assaulting women
items.description: In echoes of the high-profile Gisele Pelicot case, the National Crime Agency says it has identified more than 270 individuals linked to an online forum and its offshoots.

items.description: Around 25,000 migrants have left South Africa in recent weeks over rising anti-migrant sentiment that has resulted in the deaths of four people. One returnee from Zimbabwe recounts his experience.

items.title: South African police say anti-immigrant protests were 'mostly peaceful'
items.description: South African police said Wednesday that nationwide anti-immigration protests held the previous day were "mostly peaceful", despite isolated incidents of looting and criminal activity.

items.title: After heat wave: Wildfires ravage southern France
items.description: Firefighters battle several wildfires in southern France that were fueled by strong winds. Nearly 3,000 tourists and locals were evacuated.

items.description: The political death throes of Javier Milei's third Cabinet chief lasted longer than expected.
